text:
Admission Date:  2019-12-05              Discharge Date:   1997-07-01

Date of Birth:  2005-02-19             Sex:   F

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
E-Mycin

Attending:Kevin
Chief Complaint:
Abdominal Pain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
ERCP 1997-07-13

History of Present Illness:
Mrs. Smith is a 67 year old female with a history of crohn's
disease s/p multiple abdominal surgeries with ileostomy and
short gut syndrome and recent development of acute cholecystitis
s/p open cholecystostomy tube in 22 and ERCP with stent
placement on 2001-07-25 who presents from home with worsening
abdominal pain and nausea.   Per the surgical team she initially
underwent surgery for acute cholecystitis in 22.  The
procedure was unable to be completed secondary to significant
adhesions and was converted to an open cholecystostomy tube
placement.  Her cholecystostomy tube drained both fluid and
stones well for four weeks but subsequently migrated out and
healed over. She underwent ERCP with stent placement on 2001-07-25.
 Since procedure she has had persistent mid epigastric pain
radiating to her back with associated nausea for which she was
taking oxycodone and zofran.  Her pain increased and was
associated with worsening mental status starting at 5:30 AM on
the morning of admission.  Per her husband she was doubling over
in pain and had severe nausea and vomiting.  There was
questionable blood in her vomit.  She has had no documented
fevers or chills.  No lightheadedness, dizziness, chest pain,
difficulty breathing, change in ostomy output, dysuria,
hematuria, leg pain or swelling. All other review of systems
negative in detail.
.
In the ED, initial vs were: T:98.0 P: 74 BP: 160/72 R: 16 O2 sat
99% on RA.  She received 50 mcg fentanyl and 4 mg zofran en
route.  Blood pressures were consistently in the 200s systolic
and she received 5 mg IV metoprolol x 2 and 5 mg IV hydralazine.
 She received approximately 2 liters of IVF.  She received 40
meq potassium, ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV x 1, flagyl 500 mg IV x
1, zofran 4 mg IV x 2, morphine 4 mg IV x 2, pantoprazole 40 mg
IV x 1, unasyn 3 grams IV x 1, and ativan 1 mg IV x 1 after
which time she became slightly confused.  She also received
vitamin K 5 mg IV x 1.  She was transfused 1 unit of FFP.  EKG
showed norumal sinus rhythm, normal axis, left bund branch
block, no significant change from 1990-04-17.  Family declined NGT
placement.  She had a right upper quadrant ultrasound which
showed the common bile duct stent in place with 13 mm dilitation
and internal hypoechoic material, possibly debris or sludge.
She was evaluated by the surgical team who recommended CT scan
of the abdomen for further evaluation and admission to the Nicholas County Hospital
for close monitoring.
.
On the floor, she is currently too somnolent to respond to
questions.  She grimaces on abdominal exam.

Past Medical History:
Past Medical History:
1. Crohn's disease, as above, status post multiple surgeries
with small short bowel syndrome.
2. Recurrent small bowel obstruction.
3. Left bundle branch.
4. History of line sepsis.
5. Nephrolithiasis.
6. Osteomyelitis of the distal right femur in 1975.
7. Osteoporosis.
8. Elevated alkaline phosphatase (this was worked up and it is
felt to be of bone origin and not liver in origin).
.
Past Surgical History:
1.  Ileocolectomy in 2012.
2.  Transverse colostomy for pelvic abscesses and small bowel
obstruction in 1982.
3.  Pan proctocolectomy with ileostomy in 1990.
4.  Ileal resection (18 cm) in 1987 2 feet proximal to stoma.
5.  Lysis of adhesions 2006 complicated by wound infection and
enterocutaneous fistula.
6.  Exploratory laparotomy and lysis of adhesions with take down
of enterocutaneous fistula with small bowel resection 8/2016.
7.  Synovectomy and debridement of right distal femur in 1975.
8.  Status post tonsillectomy and adenoidectomy.
9.  Status post exploratory laparotomy and take down of
enterovesiculo cutaneous fistula 07/1998.
10. Incision and drainage of intraabdominal abscess 12/2002.
11. Status post laparotomy and lyses of adhesions and excision
on
enterocutaneous and enterovesicular fistular 12/2018.
12. Exploratory laparotomy take down of enterocutaneous fistula
small bowel resection 09/2009.
13. s/p open cholecystostomy tube 2019-04-12
14. s/p ERCP with stent placement 2001-07-25


Social History:
The patient denies tobacco and alcohol use. She is married with
two children who are healthy. She works as a homemaker.  She
lives with her husband.


Family History:
Mother with inflammatory bowel disease. Father died of heart
problems and Joseph Watts in his early 60's.


Physical Exam:
On Nicholas County Hospital Admission 2019-12-05:
Vitals: T: 99.9 BP: 170/67 P: 96 R: 22 O2: 98% on RA
General: Somolent, wakes to voice, answers simple questions, no
distress
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MM dry, oropharynx clear
Neck: supple, JVP flat, no LAD
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes, rales,
ronchi
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs,
gallops
Abdomen:  soft, diffusely tender, non-distended, bowel sounds
present, no rebound, voluntary guarding, left sided ostomy in
place with dark guaiac positive output
GU: foley draining clear urine
Ext: warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or
edema
.
On transfer to surgery 2014-08-28:
98.7  71  114/54  13  97% 2L NC
Gen: awake, alert, answering questions appropriately, no
distress
Lungs: bibasilar crackles
CV: RRR, nl S1, S2, no m/r/g
Abd: s/diffusely tender (Greatest in RLQ), +BS, no r/g
Ext: wwp, 2+ pulses, no c/c/e

Pertinent Results:
On admission 2019-12-05:
WBC-16.2*# RBC-3.28* Hgb-9.4* Hct-29.2* MCV-89 MCH-28.6
MCHC-32.1 RDW-14.0 Plt Ct-327
Neuts-87.9* Lymphs-8.0* Monos-2.8 Eos-1.0 Baso-0.2
PT-21.5* PTT-61.6* INR(PT)-2.0*
Fibrino-615*#
FDP-80-160*
Glucose-146* UreaN-10 Creat-0.8 Na-140 K-2.8* Cl-103 HCO3-24
AnGap-16
ALT-54* AST-112* AlkPhos-585* TotBili-0.7
Lipase-109*
Calcium-8.5 Phos-2.2* Mg-2.0
Hapto-311*
.
On transfer from Nicholas County Hospital to surgery (2014-08-28):
WBC-15.5* RBC-3.47* Hgb-10.6* Hct-30.9* MCV-89 MCH-30.5
MCHC-34.2 RDW-15.1 Plt Ct-233
PT-12.9 PTT-22.5 INR(PT)-1.1
Glucose-121* UreaN-20 Creat-0.9 Na-140 K-3.7 Cl-108 HCO3-26
AnGap-10
ALT-86* AST-80* AlkPhos-344* TotBili-4.8*
Calcium-7.4* Phos-2.8 Mg-2.2
.
Micro:
MRSA - positive
2019-12-05 BCx - Staph aureus, coag + - speciation pending
2019-12-05 BCx portacath - GNR
2019-12-05 BCx venipuncture - NGTD
2019-12-05 UCx - no growth
.
Imaging:
2019-12-05 RUQ US:
-Common bile duct stent in place.
-Common bile duct dilatation up to 13 mm with internal
hypoechoic material, possibly debris or sludge.
-Gallbladder contains small stones and sludge with no evidence
of
pericholecystic fluid or gallbladder wall thickening.
.
2019-12-05 CT Villa/Pelvis:
1. No evidence of acute intraabdominal or pelvic process. No
organized fluid collection identified.
2. Mild intra- and extrahepatic biliary dilatation with common
bile duct
stent in place. No filling defects identified.
3. Distended gallbladder with gallstones. No overt wall edema.
4. Mild right renal pelvicaliectasis and prominence of the right
proximal
ureter down to level of the mid pelvis adjacent to a surgical
clip.
.
EKG 2019-12-05: norumal sinus rhythm, normal axis, left bund branch
block, no significant change from 1990-04-17.
.
ERCP 1997-07-13:
-Fresh and clotted blood was seen in the major papilla.
-2 plastic stents placed in the biliary duct were found in the
major papilla. Both plastic stents were removed using a snare.
-Evidence of a previous sphincterotomy was noted in the major
papilla
-Cannulation of the biliary duct was successful and deep with a
balloon catheter using a free-hand technique.
-Filling defects were noted within the bile duct which were
consistent with blood clots.
-A balloon sweep of the bile duct yielded multiple large
clots,which were successfully extracted.
-Some fresh blood was also seen oozing from the ampulla
following clot removal.

Brief Hospital Course:
Nicholas County Hospital Course 2017-08-18
67 year old female with a history of crohn's disease s/p
multiple abdominal surgeries with ileostomy and short gut
syndrome, cholecystitis s/p open cholecystostomy tube in 22
and ERCP with stent placement on 2001-07-25 who presents from home
with worsening abdominal pain and nausea.
.
#Respiratory Failure ?????? Patient was intubated for ERCP on
1997-07-13. She was extubated 1981-04-23 and continued to saturate
well on 3-4L NC.
.
# Jennifer Edwards Pt with GPC and GNR. Pt did not meet SIRS/sepsis
criteria as she was afebrile and HR, RR were WNL. She did have
leukocytosis. Unasyn was changed to zosyn with continued
vancomycin coverage. Per surgery, pt to be treated through this
as port has been in place for 9 years with surveillance
cultures.
.
#Abdominal Pain/Leukocytosis:  Patient presented with diffuse
abdominal pain, leukocytosis with left shift, and rising LFTS.
Bilirubin initially not elevated but then increased to 2.3. Pain
developed shortly after recent ERCP 2001-07-25 which is concerning
for stent occlusion or accumulation of sludge.  Other potential
etiologies of her abdominal pain include ischemic bowel given
diffuse pain with soft abdomen and guaiac positive stools,
obstruction (less likely given exam), perforation, abscess,
biliary/peritoneal fistula. RUQ US and CT done with results
above. Pt underwent repeat ERCP 1997-07-13 with results above. She
was started on vancomycin and unasyn (changed to zosyn for GNR
bacteremia). On HD3, her abdominal pain had resolved, she had
good stool output. Pt had brief period of WBC 27-29 (entire CBC
elevated) which resolved, most likely lab error. Pt was given
fluids in case it was due to hemoconcentration and C. diff sent
although she was afebrile.
.
#Tachycardic/hypertension - Pt developed tachycardia with HR
130s-140s and SBP 170s-180s. Her tachycardia did not respond to
fluid bolus. Pt was given 5mg IV metoprolol with decrease to SBP
130s and HR 80s as there was concern she was having rebound from
holding BB. Pt's pain was also better controlled at this time,
which may have improved her symptoms.
.
#Coagulopathy:  Patient with INR of 2.0 on presentation. 1.1 at
transfer. Unclear etiology.  She has been as high as 1.6 in the
past.  Most likely etiology is nutritional deficiencies but in
this setting must be concerned for consumptive coagulopathy
(less likely given normal platelet count). She has received 5 mg
IV vitamin K and one unit of FFP. DIC labs not concerning and
INR was monitored.
.
#Guaiac positive stools:  Most likely etiology is her known
crohn's disease, but in this setting also concerned for ischemic
colitis, ulcers, other etiologies of gastrointestinal bleeding.
Patient refusing NGT.  Hematocrit currently stable. Port for
access. Pt was started on PPI IV BID and Hct monitored.
.
# Crohn's disease:  As above, s/p multiple surgeries with
ileostomy with short gut syndrome.  Pt was unable to take PO's.
PO meds were held and electrolytes closely monitored given short
gut syndrome. Unclear if steroids were given by endocrine for
adrenal insufficiency. Per pt, given by GI for Crohn's. Pt on
stress dose steroids on admission. Quick taper done to home
dose.
.
# Code:  Full (discussed with patient)
.

Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Parkview Whitley Hospital Healthcare

Discharge Diagnosis:
Cholecystitis


Discharge Condition:
Afebrile, vital signs stable; tolerating PO intake, ambulating;
ostomy output WNL


Discharge Instructions:
You came to the hospital for worsening abdominal pain and
nausea. We obtained a CT scan of your abdomen which noted a
distended gallbladder with gallstones. An ERCP was performed
which noted multiple gallstones and a balloon sweep was
performed. We started to give you a diet which you tolerated
well, and had our ostomy nurses see you during your
hospitalization.

Please continue to take your home medications as prescribed.

Please return to the hospital or closest emergency department
for abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, changes to
your ostomy output, fever greater than 101 or any other symptoms
that you may find concerning.

Followup Instructions:
You will need to follow up with Dr. Johnson in clinic to
discuss surgical management options. Please call 757-799-5748 to
schedule an appointment within 2-3 weeks. You should call to
make this appointment as soon as possible.

Please follow-up with your primary care provider [**Name Initial (PRE) 176**] 08-23
weeks regarding your hospitalization.

The following appointments have been made for you:
